sentences.text: I walk on the street in center. city.
I didn't live in the center city.
I want to go.
And they catch me, all young people.
I walking the street.
What do you mean how you doing?
I walked.
I want to see how the-- somebody said a Russian tank come to Germany.
Of course, there's a pact, Germany with Russia.
And in the street, not only Jewish boys, and take in Catholic University, but the students have a special [INAUDIBLE] and recognize them, take them, too.
And there I'm 13 month.
Take to the camp.
Take to the camp.
No.
The-- this was the army walked in the street.
And the young people taken with the-- the rifles.
And--
No, walk.
So not so far from there, maybe a half kilometer.
And take the people there.
And-- and I stayed there 13 month.
And then two officers come in and ask who understand German who--
Nothing, no.
Yes.
The army, the Polish army is there.
It looked like what with the army.
A barracks, yes, barracks.
It's clean, yeah.
But the Polish army left.
And then the German moved in.
I think I can tell you or not.
No, no.
I think about a few thousand people.
A few thousand.
I don't know how much.
Everybody think they're going to kill you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, you know, it's not so good.
They get you too much to [? full ?] to eat, you know.
And it's very bad.
No, this is a barrack.
This a long room with-- with no beds.
This is a for wood to make like-- I don't how in English--
Yeah.
Laid like this, shelf like this and that.
Hundred people on one shelf.
Yeah.
I [? want ?] [? out ?] to others, you know?
I slept in my-- as I come.
With mine-- no blanket.
No blanket.
Clothes what I had, yeah.
Nobody tell you this.
No.
The German said, you go away from-- from here.
You're going somewhere else.
You understand what I mean?
The German not tell you, nobody where you going to take you.
No.
I am the lucky. it was the two officer asked me-- not asked me, no, everybody.
Who know where they can buy [NON-ENGLISH]?
That's a [INAUDIBLE] in Poland.
[INAUDIBLE] Yeah.
But whole time, the food was very bad.
I get you-- in the morning, I get a one dry-- I don't English-- [NON-ENGLISH].
This is the dry bread.
And get your water.
You can go to the-- to take your water and [INAUDIBLE] you eat.
And that's twice a day.
Sometime get you a-- a soup.
Nobody can eat this, you know?
And this is the life in camp.
You sitting in the-- there-- inside.
No work.
Because there's a lot of places that have to clean up, heavy physical work.
And there-- I'll explain to you.
It's called in Polish a [NON-ENGLISH].
In Jewish, too, a [POLISH].
This was the length in this room.
And there are a lot of stones inside that you wash your shirts on, the shirts.
You put in there and just pressed very heavy.
A laundry.
And this, have to remove from there.
It was the half of the pump you have to remove.
And the steps, there the steps, that the Polish army when she left there, she damaged the steps.
You know, no steps to take from the second floor and the third floor down.
To take that little wood, you know, you put [INAUDIBLE].
This terrible.
You can-- lot of [INAUDIBLE] killed there.
You can't run there.
You have to walk very slowly.
If you run, kills you.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Is cold.
In the winter time--
Because it was winter by the--
In winter time it's very cold.
The Germans don't care for you if you're cold or not.
You understand what I mean?
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the shirts was everything is totally, you know [INAUDIBLE].
You have to throw out.
They sent you to take a-- a shower.
It's not a shower.
Is a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's like a steam, like a steam.
You have to steam.
You get-- you give away the [INAUDIBLE] and you cloth and steam to stay in that room, you know, to-- to make the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] clean.
Oh, yeah.
You-- you talk when-- between-- you talk, yeah.
Now, a lot of people, a lot of young people afraid to talk.
Maybe you have something there, you know, listen to you.
You know?
Everybody get a little bread and that's it.
I know what you mean.
No.
No.
Yeah.
I lost there about 25 pounds.
I'm so skinny, [LAUGHS] like a twig.
Yeah.
And for everybody the same.
It's an impossible.
And this was like a miracle.
Soldiers with-- yeah.
Soldiers.
You can't escape from there, impossible to escape.
Nobody tried.
You cannot escape.
Escape, you killed right away.
A lot of people died.
Get typhus.
You know typhus?
Other sickness, I don't know.
A lot of people killed, too.
Because you run, you try to go to the gates, killed you right away.
Yeah, I saw this.
No.
This was a lot of people in Lublin they take from the street.
Not only-- not only Jewish boys.
You know, Catholic boys take, too.
Because for him, this time, because the students from Catholic University killed 300 soldiers, [INAUDIBLE] 300 soldiers in Lublin, killed them.
The students killed them.
Now, to make [INAUDIBLE] [? lay down ?] next to the people, to the young people.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
The two German officers comes in and ask who understand German and who know a store you can buy [NON-ENGLISH].
In German, it's [NON-ENGLISH].
I tell them I know.
I know that.
I'm live in Lublin.
I born there.
And I now stores where you can find.
Not a lot of people speak German.
And take me out in center city near Krakowskie Przedmiescie Street, was called Krakowskie.
Thousands of people walk, because no cars.
In my mind had to escape, you know?
And there's a big post office.
The post office like a one block the length.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you have a exit from Krakowskie Przedmiescie Street to the hospital street.
You can go in.
And you can go out of the hospital, the [NON-ENGLISH] now in Polish.
And my mind work so fast.
And I jump in-- in the post office.
And the two German, tucked between him [INAUDIBLE].
And thousands of people on the street, we can choose nothing.
I come out of the other street, hospital street called.
And I escape to my friend, not to the house, not to my parents.
So my friend called him Torffmann, the name.
What?
Yeah, T-O-R-F-F-M-A-N.
My friend, young boys.
Two brothers there.
The people is a little richer, because the father have a horse with a wagon and make better like my father not.
And I stayed there.
And my-- I remember my father comes over [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and give me his coat, because cold.
He give me his coat, a black coat.
And the sister bring me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] black bread.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because I tell him, I [INAUDIBLE] to Russia.
I told my father and my sister I could escape to Russia.
I live 75 kilometer from the Russian border.
No.
No.
My mother cried.
I thought so.
And--
I don't know nothing.
Because there is coupons.
Everybody get a coupon to get bread and something else, you know, for the families.
And I escape.
And I take the two brothers, Torffman, with me.
I stayed three days.
And--
Yeah.
I-- when I walk out in Lublin in the road, I find other boys and together 11 boys, Jewish boys.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I walk to Chelm.
[CRYING] Chelm is 55 mile for Lublin, 55 kilometers.
Yeah.
We walk.
No.
I told my father, my sister, my mother, I not see you.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I walk [CRYING] three days [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to the border.
The border between Russia and German is a river called Bug.
God.
Bug was the river.
And this side the German.
And other side is the Russian.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember in Chelm everybody sleep over, the Jewish people, in the house--
Yeah.
In the morning, 6 o'clock, I walked.
I had over 20 kilometer to go.
And I go-- it's called Dorohusk, a town.
But everybody tell in Chelm-- Chelm us there you can come over the river to Russia.
You know, you can go over.
When I come to Dorohusk with my whole friend, the 11--
Yeah.
The German solders very friendly, the soldiers said, you not can go over.
Yesterday, closed the border.
You have to go to town called Swierce.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about 30 kilometer from Dorohusk.
This is a village.
And when I and my friends walk, it's so dark.
And a Polish farmer said, boys, don't walk.
But here, you have-- I don't know what in English what's called this.
You have when you walk you can go in on the dirt.
You sink in, yeah.
And the two farmers take the 11 boys to the house overnight.
No.
You don't have Jewish farmers.
This is-- you see, in this place it's a very poor place for the Polish people.
The farmers live very poor.
And the farmers cooked potatoes for everybody.
Yeah.
Yeah. 5:00 in the morning, I am with my friend.
We walked to the Swierce, to the-- there's the headquarters for the German.
You have to take a paper that can go over the river to Russian.
And there you have a little boat.
And the German soldiers or the Russian take you over.
If you have in the German headquarters, a [NON-ENGLISH], in German called this, you can go over.
I come there.
Said, you not can go over.
You have to go to the headquarters.
Now, what to do?
Because you have to tell him your lived before in Ukraine, in Polish Ukraine on the other side of the river, you can go.
I don't have papers.
I have papers from Lublin.
I belong to the German.
And so I go to tell him-- everybody thought I lost everything-- the bombs.
Because when I walked from Lublin to the border, the Germany killed the people in the-- in the-- in the roads.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And a little Polish boy about 12 years old comes over and said, I have a little boat. 5 zloty, that a $5-- and I had-- I take you over to the other side.
Six-- six can go in.
OK.
I don't have, but the two brothers Torffman have the money.
They pay the money.
[LAUGHS] And I take them to the river.
And nobody's there, no soldiers.
Daytime.
Daytime.
And the river is a little frozen already.
It's cold in November.
There's very cold.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Before, got in 6.
And I'm all the time they [INAUDIBLE].
I tell maybe the brothers, maybe something happened.
[INAUDIBLE] the families, go over.
Go over there.
Come back with the boat, the boy.
You take the other five together with me.
In the middle of the river, the Germans start shoot.
I jump in the water.
My luck, [INAUDIBLE] I'm not a swimmer.
I go like this in the water-- ice.
You have ice.
And thank God, I went over.
[LAUGHS]
I don't know.
Was God--
No, the water it go to there.
And there, I take my shoes off [INAUDIBLE] water.
And the stocking get frozen right away.
And stayed maybe 10 minute.
Everybody got away from there already.
Everybody is.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe 10, 15 minutes, a-- a soldier with horse, a Russian soldier, comes over, takes everybody [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to a-- a little house.
There are maybe 50 people in this house.
A lot of communists escaped from the prisons, you know.
I see straw on the floor.
And people sleep there over at night.
And one colonel said, I go send you back.
I thought if you go send me back, I will kill myself here in this place.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I called somewhere.
A lady come-- a soldier with a motorcycle.
And everybody walked and walked.
The name is the town Lubomil.
L-U-B-O-M-I-L.
Lubomil.
Russia.
No.
Just.
Yeah.
Come there.
There's the headquarters for the Russia.
They said, you can go deeper Russia or send you back.
[INAUDIBLE] before belonged to Poland, the land.
The Russian take, in the war, take, [INAUDIBLE] and make it a part-- take half Poland, Ukraine-- yeah.
And later, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe a half day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you can go.
I take your name and everything, you know, like that.
They get you a paper.
You walk.
You walk to a station.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The station, I don't remember.
And there I see a big train.
A train is there, pullman train.
You know what I what I mean, Pullman?
Not passenger.
This was--
Freight.
And there are a lot of farmers, Polish farmers.
The Russian take them all away with the cows, with the horses, everything on this train.
And I ask one farmer, how long you here?
For three days.
And my mazel-- you know what a mazel?
Mazel is luck, uh, huh.
The train, maybe an hour, the train moved.
And I jumped up the step in the wagon.
I don't-- everybody in my-- the 11 people.
And I come to Kowel.
The train.
And the train comes to Kowel.
Kowel is K-O-W-E-L.
This is, I'd say, a small city.
In Kowel, you have thousands there, ten-- tens of thousands people, young people and older people escape from Poland, to Russia, from the whole Poland, around Poland.
The people lived in the street.
It's cold.
And everybody-- I want go-- my friend, they looked here, how to sleep overnight.
And I don't know where to go.
I heard Michael.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I look.
My neighbor.
A neighbor.
From Lublin.
Said, what you do here?
And I went, the same what you do here, escape, too, no?
I told [INAUDIBLE] Mendel, the name, I don't have where to sleep.
Come with me.
He take me in a little room of above the school.
And I have 20 people in a little like my kitchen, a little place.
Now, one of brother sleep like this.
You get eat in the restaurants for nothing.
The Russian get you food for the people who escaped.
And--
Yeah.
You change now?
